comment: The City of Blue Springs is in The Kansas City Metropolitan area along Interstate 70 and US 40 Highways. This route is heavily traveled between Kansas City and St. Louis with both in State and out of State travelers. Within the City, and near the Interstate 70 corridor, there are many restaurants and retail businesses including a large shopping complex at Interstate 70 and Adams Dairy Parkway. In addition, the Blue Springs School District attracts many students and visitors who are not actual residents of the City. These daily travelers greatly affect the population levels outside of those reflected in the State population estimates of drivers 16 and over, which in turn has an impact on the disparity index. The City of Blue Springs has made a strong commitment to protect citizens, both residents and non-residents, from racially motivated or bias based profiling. In addition to the mandatory annual Racial Profiling training required by the State of Missouri, the members of the Blue Springs Police Department, both sworn and civilian, have been given additional POST certified training in this area.

The City of Bridgeton is in northwest St. Louis County with a portion of St. Louis Lambert International Airport within its boundaries. The city has a relatively low population for its 15 plus square mile footprint. There are three major highways; namely, I-70, I-270, Hwy 370, and US 67 that run through the city. Also, St. Charles Rock Road and Natural Bridge Road are major thoroughfares which also run through the City of Bridgeton. The city has a large retail/commercial/manufacturing presence within its boundaries which accounts for a large amount of non-resident motorist traveling to work within the city. These businesses include Walmart, The Home Depot, Target, Kohls, Hussmann, DePaul Hospital and Davidson to name a few. Because of the proximity to the airport, the City of Bridgeton is home to 12 hotels/motels which account for a large transient population.

As the County Seat, Clayton is home to the St. Louis County Courts, Government Center, Justice Center, and various social service agencies. The city also hosts major corporations, two universities, and a vibrant dining and retail scene, attracting visitors from across the region. As a result, Clayton's daytime population grows significantly beyond its residential base, with a substantial number of non-resident commuters and visitors. Clayton borders two diverse communities—St. Louis City and University City—further impacting the demographics of drivers encountered. Consequently, residential population figures alone are not an accurate benchmark for analyzing traffic stop disparities. Over the past two years, an increase in expired and temporary vehicle tags has contributed to a rise in traffic stops involving minority drivers. In 2024, Clayton PD received no complaints of racial profiling, and traffic stops seldom resulted in Resisting Arrest charges, reflecting the professionalism and de-escalation skills of officers. Clayton PD maintains a strong emphasis on traffic safety and public trust by providing regular training in Implicit Bias, Procedural Justice, de-escalation techniques, and guidelines discouraging arbitrary investigative or pretextual stops.

Concordia's location along I-70 and the presence of Highway 23 running through the city contribute significantly to vehicle traffic in the area. The city sees a steady influx of travelers stopping for fuel, food, or rest, which naturally increases the number of non-local vehicles on our roadways. Additionally, local factories such as Tyson Foods, Milbank Manufacturing, and Old World Spice employ many individuals who commute from outside city limits. This combination of through-traffic and commuter traffic plays a notable role in the volume and variety of vehicle stops conducted by the Concordia Police Department.

I believe that using the “residential” demographics as the metric for determining the “disparity index” for traffic stops is flawed, and can therefore lead to a flawed conclusion if drawing a conclusion based on this data. The residential demographics of Creve Coeur is not reflective of the motoring public demographics within the city limits of Creve Coeur. Creve Coeur is situated in the middle of St. Louis County, and within our city limits are some of the most utilized roadways in the metro area. Additionally, the city is the home to several major corporations and medical facilities that draw people from all over the metropolitan area, boosting the daytime population to roughly 50,000 persons. CCPD takes the issue of bias very seriously. As a CALEA-accredited agency, we have strong policies that prohibit racial bias, we provide training, supervision, and have accountability mechanisms in place to combat and address bias-based policing.

The Ellisville Police Department has one of the busiest intersections in the entire St. Louis region. In 2024, there were over nine million trips recorded on Manchester and Clarkson roads. The population data reported does not reflect the actual numbers of persons coming in or through the City of Ellisville and greatly misrepresent the number of persons in our City at any time. Our total number of stops of minority travelers is miniscule compared to the overall number of persons travelling through our community.

Gladstone is considered an “inner tier” suburb in the Kansas City Metropolitan Statistical Area. It is completely surrounded by Kansas City, MO, a densely populated urban center with significantly different demographics than Gladstone. Gladstone is primarily residential that has vibrant and active retail areas with a white population greater than 78%. These retail districts include big box stores, grocery stores and other retail businesses. The multiple major arterial roads are used by many residents of surrounding municipalities to connect to the multiple state and interstate highways that run just outside of Gladstone's city limits in addition to patronizing our businesses. When viewed from that context, it appears that at any one time, the majority of people operating vehicles inside the corporate city limits of Gladstone are not its residents. As demonstrated by submitted statistics, approximately 77% of the stops conducted by our officers are on vehicles operated by non-residents of the driving age population using your 2023 population statistic. Since the numbers clearly show the vast majority of individuals contacted by Gladstone officers during vehicle stops are not residents, comparing the demographics of these drivers to the demographics of Gladstone's residents will result in flawed conclusions. Finally, as a CALEA nationally accredited organization, the Gladstone Police Department is continuously providing training to its members on such topics as bias-based policing and legal updates to ensure a well-trained, professional police force utilizing best practices in our profession.

The City of Hazelwood encompasses approximately 16.76 square miles, making it one of the largest municipali- ties in St. Louis County, Missouri. Pursuant to the 2020 Decennial Census conducted by the United States Census Bureau, Hazelwood has a population of 25,458 residents. The city hosts more than 1,000 businesses, including 12 Fortune 500 companies, six Fortune 1000 companies, and 10 corporate headquarters. Hazelwood contains approx- imately 159.5 centerline miles of roadway, including major transportation corridors such as U.S. Highway 67 and Interstates 170, 270, and 370. It is estimated that approximately 267,000 vehicles travel through the City's corporate limits on a daily basis, evidencing its role as a significant transportation and economic hub within the region.

Something to consider when viewing these results includes: Jasper County is a crossroads of two major US Interstates (I-44, I-49), Jasper County is a thoroughfare of historic Route 66 travelled by a diverse cross section

of people from around the world. Jasper County has two major Universities (MSSU, OCC) in our County and several large corporations (Conway Trucking, Tri-State Trucking, Eagle Picher, Leggett and Platt among several other businesses) that employ people who do not live in our jurisdiction or state (Jasper County borders Kansas and Oklahoma). A lot of people traveling through our county, working in our county or going to school in our county do not reside in Jasper County. The population for Jasper County is approximately 125,056 residents, but the Chamber of Commerce estimates that on any given day there are over 220,000 people in our county.

Relying on a census population for ages 16 and over as a benchmark does not provide a realistic picture of the driving population in Kansas City, Missouri at any given time. There are several major thoroughfares that run through the City, including I-29, I-35, and I-70 that contain a large percentage of drivers not reflected in population numbers used for comparison purposes. Additionally, Kansas City is contained within a large metropolitan population, which adds to the commuter population on these roadways. When examining search rates, the probable cause/authority to search reasons should be considered. When examining arrest rates, the arrest charges should be considered. Lastly, KCPD views vehicle stop data collection as one of many tools to address the issue of racial profiling. Other tools in use by the Department include training, community programs, and policies.

The city of Lake Saint Louis is bordered by I-70 to the north and I-64 bisects the south end with the addition of Hwy 364 as a connector highway throughout St. Charles County. A large shopping center is just north of I-64 and a retail district lies directly to the south. Workers routinely travel both interstate corridors west to the Wentzville GM Plant and to major employers in O'Fallon and other points east. Additionally, the population of our city is greater than indicated from the 2020 census results of 16, 707. Based on current statistics from the 2025 World Population Review, Lake St. Louis has a population of 19,910. The city is currently growing at the rate of 3.24% and the population has increased by 18.6%. Due to these factors, calculating a disparity index based on comparison of ethnicity of drivers stopped with the ethnicity of the city's population may be misleading due to the transient traffic.

Maryland Heights has 1,880 businesses, which employ 43,257 people. Maryland Heights is the host city to Hollywood Casino, which had 3,640,831 visitors in 2024. The 20,000 seat Verizon Wireless Amphitheater had 510,057 visitors in 2024. The 4,500 seat Centene St Louis Music Park had 86,875 visitors in 2024. The Centene Ice Rink had 825,217 visitors in 2023. The city has 26 hotels with 4,000 rooms. Maryland Heights has 82 liquor establishments selling alcohol by the drink. Finally, the section of I-270 in Maryland Heights has an average daily traffic count reported by MoDOT of 138,200 vehicles. The annual demographics of the 50,443,000 vehicles on I-270; the 3,640,831 visitors to the casino; the 510,057 visitors to the amphitheater; The combined total of 912,092 patrons of the Centene Community Ice Center; and the 43,257 people employed in Maryland Heights are not known and impact the validity of this report.
The New Haven PD disbanded in February of 2024 and did not start back up until I took over on January 1,2025.

The City of O'Fallon is located in the center of St. Charles County. We have 2 major Interstates- I-70/64, Route 364 and State Highway 79. O'Fallon also has a major arterial roadway- MO K- that connects the 4 highways from north to south and is a major retail corridor. Because of these heavily traveled roadways, our driver population is more diverse than the census population would indicate, making our disparity index artificially high.

The Riverside Police Department, by policy and practice, prohibts racial profiling. Some factors dramatically increase the driving population above Riverside's citied driver-eligible population. Those factors include a daytime population that grows by 87%; the presence of a riverboat casino; bordering on two major metropolitan cities (Kansas City, Mo and Kansas City, KS); and state and federal highway routes traveling through the City with a daily traffic count approaching 300,000 vechiles.

We acknowledge the importance of reviewing and understanding traffic stop data, especially when disparities appear to be present. After an initial review of the findings, it is important to consider a number of contextual factors that contribute to these statistics. Daily, the City of St. John experiences a notable increase in overall vehicle traffic, particularly in key corridors that serve as transit routes for local and regional commuters and commercial transport. This influx leads to a higher volume of vehicle stops citywide, some of which correlate with changes in traffic patterns, event-driven surges, and ongoing infrastructure projects diverting traffic into specific neighborhoods. We understand that it is critical to ensure that traffic enforcement efforts remain fair, consistent, and driven by objective criteria. We also recognize the importance of community trust and remain committed to ensure that enforcement is in the best interest of community safety and aligned with best practices.

As a law enforcement agency at Lake of the Ozarks, a popular tourist destination, the Sunrise Beach Police De- partment operates in an environment where daily traffic volumes far exceed what might be expected for a community of our size. Our roadways experience a substantial amount of visitor traffic, particularly during peak seasons. As a result, local traffic counts and associated enforcement activity are not solely indicative of our residential population, but rather the unique demands of a high-traffic tourist area.

Winona Police Department has previously been unable to provide more detail on traffic stops as the system we use for ticket writing has only recently provided more detailed options when entering traffic stops.

I would like it noted that our population per the census in 2021 was 3116. Not the 2354 stated in your report.

Hwy. 171, which is a major highway connecting Southeast Kansas and Southwest Missouri, goes through our city. It connects with Hwy. 49 to Arkansas, and Hwy. 71 to Kansas City, It is also the main road between Pittsburg State University in Kansas to Joplin, Missouri. Due to this corridor there is a much larger percentage of all races/ethnicities passing through that is not reflected in our city population.

As the County Seat, Clayton is home to the St. Louis County courts, Government Center, the Justice Center, and many other social services. Clayton is also home to major corporations, two universities, and a destination for restaurants and retail businesses. As a hub of economic, governmental, and educational activity for the entire region, Clayton welcomes diverse visitors to the community. The daytime population of Clayton increases substantially due to these activities and amenities. Clayton also borders two diverse communities in St. Louis City and University City. Based on these characteristics, the use of Clayton's residential population is an inadequate method for measuring disparities in traffic stops. In 2023, no complaints of racial profiling were received against Clayton officers. Traffic stops in Clayton also resulted in very few Resisting Arrest charges, which validates the officers' professional demeanor and ability to de-escalate. Additionally, Clayton officers receive training to prevent the arbitrary use of investigative stops, pretextual stops, consent searches, and intrusive questions. Clayton officers also receive training in Implicit

Bias and Procedural Justice.

In regards to the 2023 Vehicle Stops Report, I wish to provide the following information to help the reader better understand the data as it relates to the City of Des Peres. The residential demographics of Des Peres is not reflective of the motoring public demographics within the city limits. The City of Des Peres is the crossroads for two of the busiest highways in St. Louis County, Interstate 270 and Manchester Rd (Highway 100). In addition, our community is a major retail hub serving people throughout the region, vastly increasing our population day to day. Determining the racial makeup of the motoring public in Des Peres is very complex, thus the true relevant data would be the comparison of traffic stops with the racial diversity of the St. Louis County population in general. Our department does not engage in discriminatory practices, and the department, and city, would not tolerate it if it were to occur. Any reference to the contrary due to the data compiled in the current manner for the Attorney General's report is unwarranted and erroneous.

Some factors to consider when looking at the Jasper County Sheriff's Office index are as follows: Jasper County is a crossroads of two major US Interstates (I-44, I-49). We are also a thoroughfare of historic Route 66 travelled by a diverse cross section of people from around the world. We have 2 major Universities (MSSU, OCC) in our County and several large corporations (Conway Trucking, Tri-State Trucking, Eagle Picher, Leggett and Platt among several other businesses that employ people who do not live in our jurisdiction or state. (We border Kansas and Oklahoma) We also have a large mall that is in Jasper County. A lot of people traveling through our county, working in our county or going to school in our county do not reside in Jasper County. Our population for Jasper County is approximately 124,075 residence but the Chamber of Commerce estimates that on any given day there are over 200,000 people in our county.

The population “estimates” traditionally used in the Vehicle Stop Report (VSR) are not a true reflection of the racial diversity represented in Ladue on any given day and do not take into consideration the population of the surrounding area. As stated in the Attorney General's report, ”the Disparity Index is highly problematic because: i) it is sensitive to the choice of the benchmark population; ii) as a ratio, it is affected by changes in both the numerator and denominator; and iii) it is not meaningful to compare the Disparity Index across communities with different percentages of minority populations.” Ladue is home to several major thoroughfares, which are used to connect Ladue to people from all over St. Louis City, St. Louis County, and throughout Missouri. I-170 and US 64/40 both travel through Ladue, along with several city streets which all carry tens of thousands of vehicles through Ladue daily. The transitory population is far contrasting to the residential population and is more reflective of the demographics of this State and St. Louis County. Our concern is that the residential data used in the VSR varies greatly and does not provide an accurate account of the diverse transitory population that truly represents the diversity within the City of Ladue at any given time. Simply using St. Louis County, the State of Missouri, or any surrounding jurisdiction's population percentages would drastically change the outlook of the Disparity Index and would more accurately reflect our department's continued commitment to fair and impartial policing.

We also have a major Interstate highway running through the middle of town and a large truck stop directly off the interstate. Daily, these things bring more diverse motorists through and into Peculiar than is represented in our population. Comparisons of stops to population demographics are of limited value, and should only be used for limited inferences.

comment: Mapping and navigational programs route motorists through the City of Weatherby Lake on two thoroughfares that connect to larger cities and roadways. Traffic studies have shown a significant yearly increase of automobiles that travel these thoroughfares on a daily basis. These motorists reflect a large number of non-resident drivers from outside the jurisdiction.
According to the July 1, 2022 census report we have 16,177 instead of the 12,000 that is reflected in the report. This drastically changes the report.

Our City borders multiple other municipalities and jurisdictions, which are interconnected with the remaining 80-90 specifically identifiable areas within St. Louis County. Transient motorists are a commonality on the state, county, and municipal roadway systems within our City making it a major crossroads that is consistently accessed by non- residents. During this period, Non-Resident Stops represented approximately 82% of the total vehicle stops in our City (Table 1). The Location of Stop is primarily on County roadways at a total rate of 65%. County roadways are the major thoroughfares used to pass through our jurisdiction by non-residents (Table 4). This belief is also supported by Table 2, whereas 82% of the total stops (White 95%, Black 81%, Hispanic 100%, and Other 95%) were not residents of the City at the time of the stop. Information gathered on resident, race, gender, and age status may be misrepresented by the driver at the time of the stop. The data does not include measurable information such as 1) the volume of total non-resident motorists that were not stopped during this period, 2) the volume of individual ethnic, gender, and age groups among the volume of the total non-resident motorist group and 3) the exclusion of motorists who do not have a driver's license which implies that every individual age 16+ has a driver's license and drives a vehicle. This also excludes individuals between ages 15 and 16 that are permitted by law to obtain a driver's license permit to drive a vehicle. The Vehicle Stops Report is open to extremely broad and very shallow conclusions as the interpretations of the outcomes provided cannot give rise to any legitimate and credible argument of such since this can neither be proven or disproven. The margin for error in the report has a high probability for

misrepresentation in its findings.

The population estimate used to analyze the 2022 Boone County Sheriff's Office's traffic stop data was 149,127, while the U.S. Census Bureau estimated Boone County's population to be 187,690 in 2022.

Boone County is a regional employment hub in mid-Missouri and home to numerous college campuses, numerous hospitals, clinics, businesses, sporting events, and entertainment venues. Visitors are drawn to Boone County result- ing in temporary population increases at various times throughout each year. Boone County also has the convergence

of two major highways (Interstate 70 & U.S. Highway 63).

In 2022, 91% of the time deputies didn't know the driver's race prior to the traffic stop and approximately 30% of drivers stopped were non-residents. The US Census Bureau reports the demographic makeup by race in Boone County very differently than what was used for the benchmark to figure the disparity index by race. For example, the US Census Bureau indicates the following percentages for each race and the percentage used to compute the disparity index is in parenthesis: White alone – 78.0% (82.08%); Black or African American alone – 10.0% (8.53%); Hispanic or Latino – 3.7% (3.16%); American Indian and Alaska Native alone – 0.5% (0.24% for only Native American); Asian alone – 5.0% (4.69%). Finally, 3.5% of Boone County's population identify as “Two or More Races” and may appear to be a part of any race listed.

Racial disparity based on raw traffic stop data in comparison to the local population is not indicative of any underlying issues absent consideration of other socio-economic factors. As a CALEA accredited agency, we continually train our staff on unbiased policing and recognizing bias. We also continually evaluate enforcement activities, disparity numbers, and complaints to identify potential areas for improvement.

The City of Bridgeton is in northwest St. Louis County with a portion of St. Louis Lambert International Airport within its boundaries. The city has a relatively low population for its 15 plus square mile footprint. There are three major highways; namely, I-70, I-270, Hwy 370, and US 67 that run through the city. Also, St. Charles Rock Road and Natural Bridge Road are major thoroughfares which also run through the City of Bridgeton. The city has a large retail/commercial/manufacturing presence within its boundaries which accounts for a large amount of non-resident motorist traveling to work within the city. These businesses include Walmart, The Home Depot, Target, Kohls, Hussmann, DePaul Hospital and Davidson to name a few. Because of the proximity to the airport, the City of Bridgeton is home to 12 hotels/motels which account for a large transient population.

A large portion of our agency's traffic stops occur on Hwy. 171 which is a major highway connecting Southeast Kansas and Southwest Missouri, Hwy. 49 to Arkansas, and Hwy. 71 to Kansas City. It also provides the main road between Pittsburg State University in Kansas to Joplin, Missouri. This also makes it a popular corridor for drug transportation between the states. Also, I believe there is an assumption that the officers know the race of the driver of the vehicle before it is stopped in your data collection. The truth is there is an average 13.5 hours of daylight during the year, it is very difficult, if not impossible to tell the race of drivers during night time hours, and it is my observation that it is not always possible to determine the race of a driver before a traffic stop during daylight hours. There is a 'time of stop” space on the traffic stop data sheet, but it is not asked for on the report. Due to this corridor, and the amount of our traffic stops on that roadway, there is a much larger range and percentage of all races/ethnicities passing through that is not reflected in our city population. I believe that due to this, it skews our VSR report when looking at our population statistics only.

The most common error found in these reports is the use of Census data as the benchmark for comparing the racial makeup of the jurisdiction to the racial makeup of those drivers stopped by the police. When people get into their car they do not limit their travels to their city limits and Census data does not demonstrate who is on the road or who is committing traffic violations.

The Ellisville Police Department is a small 10,000 resident community in West St. Louis County. While our population is fairly low, we have two of the largest major thoroughfares in the St. Louis Region. Manchester Road and Clarkson Road sees 1.2 million drivers travel our roadway and pass through Ellisville every month. The numbers displayed, while reasonable by all counts, do not accurately reflect the diversity of drivers travelling through our community. We are confident that the interactions we have with all persons in our community meet the highest standards of professional policing and appropriate enforcement.

Independence is the fifth-largest city in Missouri, county seat and immediate suburb of Kansas City, with a popu- lation of 117,217. Ten major Interstates, highways and roadways allow direct access to the multiple racially diverse Kansas City metropolitan attractions located in the community; Harry S. Truman Presidential Library and historic home, Silverstein Eye Centers Arena , Super-Regional Independence Center Shopping Mall (2nd largest in KC Metro area with approximately 12 million annual visitors), 39th Street retail area, AMC 20 movie theater complex, Crysler Baseball Stadium, Community of Christ International Headquarters, colleges, community festivals, and multi-cultural dining venues. For 2022, Resident stops were 6,582. . . Non-Resident stops were 7,268 for a total stops of13,850. So, this was over 50% that were nonresidents. Significant traffic safety efforts are conducted along the major roadways and additional funding is provided for enforcement through state funded Hazardous Moving Violation grants, DWI Enforcement grants and Occupant Protection grants.

Due to the reduced manpower, modified COVID schedules, and modified COVID work/activity procedures sig- nificantly impacted 2020 and 2021' s data the 2022 data is compared to 2019 as this is a more accurate representation rather than the previous two years. City of Independence, 2022 disparity index for black, Hispanic and Asian drivers dropped after remaining fairly consistent over the previous five years. Black driver disproportions dropped from 3.63 in 2019 to 2.5 in 2022. The metropolitan attractions and thirty-one low income housing apartment complexes have influenced the racial composition of the motoring public to the community. The Independence Police Department is a professional law enforcement organization that is committed to impartial, competent, and courteous service to the public without regard to race, creed or gender. Officers receive annual training on cultural challenges and issues by ensuring and maintaining accountability in part through the integrity of the racial profiling documentation process in compliance with Missouri State Statute.

Disparity indexes have not fluctuated significantly since the data collection began in 2000. However, these numbers should be interpreted cautiously. Specifically, relying on a census population for ages 16 and over as a benchmark does not provide a realistic picture of the driving population in Kansas City, Missouri at any given time. There are several major thoroughfares that run through the City, including I-29, I-35, and I-70 that contain a large percentage of drivers not reflected in the static estimated census population used for comparison purposes. Additionally, Kansas City is contained within a metropolitan population of approximately 2.1 million, which adds to the commuter population on these roadways. When examining search rates, the probable cause/authority to search reasons should be considered. When examining arrest rates, the arrest charges should be considered. Lastly, KCPD views vehicle stop data collection as one of many tools to address the issue of racial profiling. Other tools in use by the Department include training, community programs, and policies.

Kirksville is home to two major universities whose enrollment data is not totally reflected in the census figure. Truman State University has 455 (11.5%) students listed other than white. They also have an additional 293 (7.4%) International students according to the 2022-2023 enrollment statistics. ATSU enrollment for the fall of 2022 has

The population “estimates” traditionally used in the Vehicle Stop Report (VSR) are not a true reflection of the racial diversity represented in Ladue on any given day and do not take into consideration the population of the surrounding area. As stated in the Attorney General's report, ”the Disparity Index is highly problematic because: i) it is sensitive to the choice of the benchmark population; ii) as a ratio, it is affected by changes in both the numerator and denominator; and iii) it is not meaningful to compare the Disparity Index across communities with different percentages of minority populations.”

Ladue is home to several major thoroughfares, which are used to connect Ladue to people from all over St. Louis City, St. Louis County, and throughout Missouri. I-170 and US 64/40 both travel through Ladue, along with

several city streets which all carry tens of thousands of vehicles through Ladue daily. The transitory population is far contrasting to the residential population and is more reflective of the demographics of this State and St. Louis County.

Our concern is that the residential data used in the VSR varies greatly and does not provide an accurate account of the diverse transitory population that truly represents the diversity within the City of Ladue at any given time. Simply using St. Louis County, the State of Missouri, or any surrounding jurisdiction's population percentages would drastically change the outlook of the Disparity Index and would more accurately reflect our department's continued commitment to fair and impartial policing.

The city of Lake Saint Louis is bordered by I-70 to the north and I-64 bisects the south end with a recent addition of Hwy 364 as a new connector highway throughout St. Charles County. A large shopping center is just north of I-64 and a retail district lies directly to the south. Workers routinely travel both interstate corridors west to the Wentzville GM Plant and to major employers in O'Fallon and other points east. Additionally, the population of our city is greater than indicated from the 2020 census results. As of 2020, the population of the city shows 16,864. Due to these factors, calculating a disparity index based on comparison of ethnicity of drivers stopped with the ethnicity of the city's population may be misleading due to the transient traffic.

The rapid growth of Lee's Summit is outpacing the ability of census data to track the City's population of all demographics. As a local example, the data underrepresents the diversity of the population, reporting 7.85% African American residents while the school district reports 12.6% of students are African American, almost a 5% difference. Lee's Summit is also a retail, education and employment destination, attracting residents from adjacent urban centers with higher minority population percentages (25 – 40%) not reflected in the population data. The driving population statistics reflect traffic on major arterial roadways, such as I-470, M-291, US-50, and M-150. This through traffic is illustrated by the fact that over half of all individuals stopped are non-residents. The highest statistically significant search rate indicates African Americans are searched at a factor of 3.56, but this includes 148 “searches” mandated incident to or inventory due to arrest or the seizure of contraband in plain view. Only ten searches were based upon consent, along with 32 for reasonable suspicion (smell of alcohol or drugs or suspicion of a weapon) out of 3,849 traffic stops, or 1%. The police department continues to select, hire and train a diverse workforce to reduce the potential

for bias in policing.

Maryland Heights has 1900 businesses, which employ 41,776 people. Maryland Heights is the host city to Hollywood Casino, which had 3,784,546 visitors in 2022. The 20,000 seat Verizon Wireless Amphitheater had 398,178 visitors in 2022. The city has 22 hotels with 3,706 rooms. Maryland Heights has 60 liquor establishments selling alcohol by the drink. Finally, the section of I-270 in Maryland Heights has an average daily traffic count reported by MoDOT of 134,436 vehicles. The annual demographics of the 49,069,140 vehicles on I-270; the 3,784,546 visitors to the casino; the 398,178 visitors to the amphitheater and the 41,776 people employed in Maryland Heights are not known and impact the validity of this report.

agency: Missouri Dept of Conservation Protection Branch
Missouri Western State University Police Dept
NW MO State University Police Dept

comment: The Platte County Sheriff's Office provides patrol and traffic enforcement services in an area that includes multiple major interstate corridors, a large shopping and entertainment district, casino, many hotels, and the Kansas City International Airport. It is bordered by Kansas City, KS, Kansas City, MO, and Leavenworth, KS, with minority populations of 49.0%, 40.3%, and 25.3%, respectively. It should be noted that 70% of all 2022 traffic stops by Platte County personnel, were reported as nonresident drivers. Although the racial makeup of all of the nonresident drivers traveling Platte County roadways is unknown, it is unlikely to accurately reflect the racial and ethnic makeup of the county's population. The Platte County Sheriff's Office prohibits racial profiling by policy and is committed to ensuring impartial public safety service regardless of race, age, gender or other factors. Officers receive annual racial and cultural sensitivity training and the Sheriff's Office maintains accountability through the racial profiling reporting process in compliance with Missouri State Statute. Data included in the Platte County report includes stops conducted in unincorporated Platte County and the jurisdictions of Camden Point, Dearborn, Houston Lake, and Lake Waukomis.

Our city has a small population of 429 residents, which is not representative of the diverse demographic composition of the over forty million vehicles traveling through our city annually. These motorists are primarily en-route to entertainment districts, shopping areas, hotels, restaurants, multifamily housing complexes, and the Kansas City International Airport. We have an Interstate and four other major traffic arteries that intersect our city. The demographic composition of drivers at any given time may not reflect our local population, contributing to the disparity in the index. We continually work diligently to ensure equitable policing practices that promote the safety and well-being of all that live, work and visit our city. We are dedicated to building trust between our agency and

the community.

Population of Republic Missouri according to the United States Census is 19,136. Population Population Estimates, July 1, 2021, (V2021) 19,136 Population estimates base, April 1, 2020, (V2021) 18,680 Population, Census, April 1, 2020 18,750 Population, Census, April 1, 2010 14,751 Race and Hispanic Origin White alone, percent 92.3% Black or African American alone, percent(a) 1.1% American Indian and Alaska Native alone, percent(a) 0.1% Asian alone, percent(a) 1.3% Native Hawaiian and Other Pacific Islander alone, percent(a) 0.0% Two or More Races, percent 3.6% Hispanic or Latino, percent(b) 3.7% White alone, not Hispanic or Latino, percent 90.9%

https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/fact/table/republiccitymissouri/PST045222

The Vehicle Stops Report compares the race of drivers in all traffic stops made by a police agency to the racial makeup of that agency's population. Richmond Heights' African American population is under 9% of the total population of the City. Richmond Heights borders a major metropolitan city with a near 50% African American makeup while residing in a county with nearly a 25% African American population. It stands to reason that our Department's disparity index is mathematically skewed by this.

The Riverside Police Department, by policy and practice, prohibits racial profiling. Some factors dramatically increase the driving population above Riverside's cited driver-eligible population. Those factors include a daytime population that grows by 87%; the presence of a riverboat casino; bordering on two major metropolitan cities (Kansas City, MO and Kansas City, KS); and state and federal highway routes traveling through the City with a daily traffic count approaching 300,000 vehicles.

agency: SE MO State University-Dept of Public Safety
St. Charles City Parks and Recreation Dept
St. Charles Community College Police Dept

comment: In reference to our “Vehicle Stops Report”, I would wish to advise your office that the City of St. John, MO experiences, on average, more than 28,000 additional non-resident persons traveling through our jurisdiction on a daily basis. The influx of individuals are either passing through our City due to travels in or out from Lambert International Airport, commuting to their work location, and/or visiting our St. John Plaza shopping center. I understand that our “Stops” statistic percentages do not reflect an accurate comparison to our current census demographic data; however, the aforementioned gives reason for the accounted off-set. Thank you for your review and consideration.

The disparity index is based upon the population demographics of the City of Town & Country, which has a current population of 11,640 (disparity index only indicates 9509) with an African American population of .9% (US Census Bureau). This does not accurately reflect the demographics of the true motoring population through the City daily. Every weekday, nearly 415,000 motorists travel through our city on the 2nd most voluminous interchange in the State (Interstates 64/40/270). Our motoring public is more reflective of the demographics of the State and St. Louis County, which are comprised of African American populations of 11.4% and 24.2% respectively (US Census Bureau). The stop data for Town & Country Police indicate over 71% of stops occuned on either the interstates, US Highway

or State Highway, of which, 83% were initiated for hazardous moving violations. Overall, only 23% of the stops were African American drivers. The natural implication of this statistic is that the ratio of the race of motorists stopped for violations would reflect state and county demographics, not the residential population of the City of Town & Country.

agency: University of Missouri-Kansas City Police Dept
University of Missouri-St. Louis Police Dept

comment: According to the United States Census Bureau the estimated population of Waynesville as of, April 1 st, 2020, is 5406. Major routes through Waynesville are Interstate 44 and US Route 66, that exists as a scenic route through the entirety of Waynesville. Route 17, crosses Interstate 44 at exit 153 at Buckhorn and turns north to Crocker. South of Interstate 44, Highway 17 hugs the western edge of Fort Leonard Wood, near Laquey, and joins Highway 32 in Roby. Highway T runs north from Highway 17 at Waynesville to Swedeborg. Waynesville, St. Robert, and Fort Leonard Wood along with their surrounding rural areas are served by the Way- nesville R-VI School District which is by far the largest in the county. There are currently 5821 students and 874 staff members at the school daily. The City of Waynesville borders Fort Leonard Woods West Gate.- Fort Leonard Woods population per the US Census Bureau was 15,959 at the 2020 census. The racial makeup of the CDP as of July 1,2019 (V2019) was 60.0% White alone, 16.5% Black or African American alone, 2.8% American Indian, 4.7% Asian alone, 0.4% Hawaiian or Pacific Islander, Hispanic or Latino of any race were 23.3% of the population. I believe all the information presented may provide additional information to clarify the data and provide a more accurate reflection of the demographics of our driving population. I hope this information will improve our ability to provide a more balanced report to the public.

An influx of international minorities due to employment opportunities in and around our jurisdiction which increased traffic flow and number of drivers with diverse cultural groups within this jurisdiction.

Our Car stops increased again in 2021. This is due to more call volume, more criminal activity in the County, combined with our proactive patrolling, and further lifting of COVID restrictions. Our car stops have increased across all races, both genders, age groups and the locations of the stops.

In 2014, Ashland Police Department administration enacted policy prohibiting Ashland police officers from conduct- ing traffic enforcement on U.S. Highway 63 which travels through the city limits of Ashland, Missouri. In May of 2020, the prohibition against traffic enforcement on U.S. Highway 63 was lifted, which resulted in a substantial shift in where traffic enforcement contacts were made compared to the previous 6.5 years. Ashland is equidistant between Columbia, Missouri and Jefferson City, Missouri, thus the vast majority of traffic enforcement contacts made along U.S. Highway 63 by Ashland Police represents motorists who are traveling through Ashland, Missouri. Our primary focus on U.S. Highway 63 are hazardous moving violations, particularly excessive speed (at least 15 over the speed limit).

In 2021, the Aurora and Marionville Police Department experienced low staffing levels and equipment concerns that led to lower than expected traffic enforcement data. In the late months of 2021, the department acquired new radar systems for the patrol vehicles. Previously, the department only had a few units equipped with radar systems which did not function well. Additionally, in late 2021, the AMPD became fully staffed, increasing the number of officers on the street. Proactive traffic enforcement is also a priority for the new administration. Therefore, traffic enforcement data would have seen an increase in late 2021 and the numbers are expected to be considerably higher in 2022 with changes made internally to the department.

Our City borders multiple other municipalities and jurisdictions, which are interconnected with the remaining 80-90 specifically identifiable areas within St. Louis County. Transient motorists are a commonality on the state, county, and municipal roadway systems within our City making it a major crossroads that is consistently accessed by non- residents. During this period, Non-Resident Stops represented approximately 89% of the total vehicle stops in our City (Table 4). The Location of Stop is primarily on County roadways at a total rate of 61%. County roadways are the major thoroughfares used to pass through our jurisdiction by non-residents (Table 4). This belief is also supported by Table 2, whereas 89% of the total stops (White 90%, Black 89%, Native American 100%, and Other 91%) were not residents of the City at the time of the stop. Information gathered on resident, ethic, gender, and age status may be misrepresented by the driver at the time of the stop. The data does not include measurable information such as 1) the volume of total non-resident motorists and 2) the volume of individual ethnic groups, gender, and age groups among the volume of the total non-resident motorist group and 3) the exclusion of motorists who do not have a driver's license which implies that every individual age 16+ has a driver's license and drives a vehicle. This also excludes individuals between ages 15 and 16 that are permitted by law to obtain a driver's license permit to drive

a vehicle. The Vehicle Stops Report is open to extremely broad and very shallow conclusions as the interpretations of the outcomes provided cannot give rise to any legitimate and credible argument of such since this can neither be proven or disproven. The margin for error in the report has a high probability for misrepresentation in its findings.
The city of Bertrand is a bedroom community that services two cities one on either side. Due to this a large number of vehicles that travel through the city are not local.

The City of Blue Springs is located in The Kansas City Metropolitan area along Interstate 70 and US 40 Highways. This route is heavily traveled between Kansas City and St. Louis with both in State and out of State travelers. Within the City, and in close proximity to the Interstate 70 corridor, are many restaurants and retail businesses to include a large shopping complex at Interstate 70 and Adams Dairy Parkway. In addition, the Blue Springs School District attracts a large number of students and visitors who are not actual residents of the City. These daily travelers greatly affect the population levels outside of those reflected in the State population estimates of drivers 16 and over, which in turn has an impact on the disparity index. The City of Blue Springs has made a strong commitment to protect citizens, both residents and non-residents, from racial or bias based profiling. In addition to the mandatory annual Racial Profiling training required by the State of Missouri, the members of the Blue Springs Police Department, both sworn and civilian, have been given additional POST certified training in this area.

The population used for analysis of the 2021 BCSO traffic stop data was 147,343, which is roughly about 36,000 less than the actual population of Boone County. The U.S. Census Bureau states Boone County's 2020 population was 183,610 and estimates that increased to 185,840 in 2021. Within Missouri, Boone County is the 8th most populated county and the City of Columbia, the County seat, is the 4th most populated city. Boone County, the regional employment hub in mid-Missouri, is also the home to MU, Stephens College, Columbia College and several satellite college campuses. With numerous hospitals, medical clinics, businesses, sporting events, and entertainment venues, visitors from around the state and country are drawn to Boone County and the population increases dras- tically on weekends and during daytime hours with commuting workers, students, and visitors. Boone County has the convergence of two major highways (Interstate 70 and U.S. Highway 63). In 2021, deputies did not know the race of the driver prior to the stop during 85% of the traffic stops. Additionally, 23% of motorists stopped in 2021 were non-residents simply visiting or passing through Boone County. Racial disparity based on raw traffic stop data in comparison to the local population is not indicative of any underlying issues absent consideration of other socio- economic factors. As a CALEA accredited agency, we continually train our staff on unbiased policing and recognizing bias. We also continually evaluate enforcement activities, disparity numbers, and complaints to determine if there are areas in which we need to improve.

The City of Branson is located in the hear of the Ozarks and has been a national tourist destination for decades. The Tri-Lakes Area Convention and Visitors Bureau estimate the City of Branson welcomed some 10 million visitors in 2021. That number surpoasses previous year estimates by some 500,000 people. The majority of Branson tourists arice from within a three/four hour drive. The city regularly sees tourists license plates from all 48 continental states. The 2020 US Census positions resident population at 11,906 which maarked a 0.78% growth over 2010. Branson residents clearly represent a very snall portion of our annual driving population. Over one third of all 2021 Branson Police Department traffic stops were non-resident drivers. These factors impact our annual vehicle stop data.

The City of Branson West is a small town with a population of approximately 400 residence with two major Missouri highways that run through it and has a large number of drivers from outside our jurisdiction that pass through going to Branson and Table Rock Lake.

The City of Bridgeton is in northwest St. Louis County with a portion of St. Louis Lambert International Airport within its boundaries. The city has a relatively low population for its 15 plus square mile footprint. There are three major highways; namely, I-70, I-270, Hwy 370, and US 67 that run through the city. Also, St. Charles Rock Road and Natural Bridge Road are major thoroughfares which also run through the City of Bridgeton. The city has a large retail/commercial/manufacturing presence within its boundaries which accounts for a large amount of non-resident motorist traveling to work within the city. These businesses include Walmart, The Home Depot, Target, Kohls, Hussmann, DePaul Hospital and Davidson to name a few. Because of the proximity to the airport, the City of Bridgeton is home to 12 hotels/motels which account for a large transient popula- tion.

I would like to address the accuracy of the information being provided on our police departments vehicle stop re- port. First, our total city population listed for 2020 is 2341 in the Vehicle Stop Report. According to the website https://datausa.io/profile/geo/byrnes-mill-mo our population in 2019 was 2970. That is well over a 20% difference in population which I feel would significantly change our numbers in the listed Rates category. Second, our total listed Black population for 2020 is 0. I can't find contradictory information on any website but know that we have at least 12 Black residents in our city. I also know that this number will not have much impact on our numbers listed in the Rates category but wanted it noted none the less. Third, our city is traversed in its entire length by a heavily travelled 4 lane divided state highway. This highway produces a large majority of our citations, searches, and contraband hit rates from traffic stops and traffic crashes.

The Sheriff's Office racial profiling stats may be skewed for several reasons. Cape Girardeau County is the largest population center in Southeast Missouri. People consistently travel to Cape Girardeau County for work, shopping, and recreational opportunities. Southeast Missouri State University and its diverse population of students is located within Cape Girardeau County. A large number of these students are from other areas and moved to Cape Girardeau to attend school on campus. Interstate 55 runs through Cape Girardeau County. Motorists from all over the state and country use the highway daily for travel.

A large portion of our agency's traffic stops occur on Hwy. 171 which is a major highway connecting Southeast Kansas and Southwest Missouri, Hwy. 49 to Arkansas, and Hwy. 71 to Kansas City. It also provides the main road between Pittsburg State University in Kansas to Joplin, Missouri. This also makes it a popular corridor for drug transportation between the states. Also, I believe there is an assumption that the officers know the race of the driver of the vehicle before it is stopped in your data collection. The truth is there is an average 13.5 hours of daylight during the year, it is very difficult, if not impossible to tell the race of drivers during night time hours, and it is my observation that it is not always possible to determine the race of a driver before a traffic stop during daylight hours. There is a 'time of stop” space on the traffic stop data sheet, but it is not asked for on the report. Due to this corridor, and the amount of our traffic stops on that roadway, there is a much larger range and percentage of all races/ethnicities passing through that is not reflected in our city population. I believe that due to this, it skews our VSR report when looking at our population statistics only.

The Chesterfield Police Department strictly prohibits any and all forms of biased policing (racial profiling) by its per- sonnel. This applies to all encounters between police officers and citizens including arrests, traffic contacts, suspicious vehicle stops, field contacts, investigative detentions, consensual inquiries and forfeiture efforts. The department has implemented a number of safeguards in order to prevent and or detect any possible signs of biased policing. The de- partment utilizes monthly Quality Service Evaluations (QSE); randomly choosing sixty individuals who had contact with the agency's personnel each month and mailing them survey forms regarding the quality of services received. A more extensive citizen survey is mailed to every household in Chesterfield once every two years seeking similar input. Annual training on biased based policing is provided to all commissioned personnel and a Citizens' Concerns memo is compiled quarterly and reviewed by command personnel. Another means of preventing and monitoring for biased policing practices is through the department's weekly in-car and body worn camera audits which are conducted by all supervisors in the Patrol, Traffic & Special Enforcement Units. Each officer in these units has a standard number of their vehicle stops randomly chosen and reviewed by their supervisors each month. The results of these audits are forwarded to the division commander for review. It should be noted that this data regarding the percentage of African-Americans living in Chesterfield may be somewhat deceiving as the day-time population of Chesterfield, in both numbers and racial/ethnic makeup, is considerably different than those portrayed by the latest Census figures. Because Chesterfield is home to four major retails centers, numerous restaurants and a large light-industrial center, the numbers of African-Americans working, shopping and otherwise doing business within the city limits is likely much higher than is represented in the City's population. Additionally, Chesterfield has an interstate (I-64) and several large state and county roadways that intersect it and although the racial make-up of those traveling on these

roadways is unknown, it is very unlikely that it would closely mirror the racial and ethnic makeup of the city's population. The Chesterfield Police Department is committed to investigating all complaints of biased policing and conducts an annual administrative review.

In an analysis of the Christian County Sheriff's Office 2021 Vehicles Stops Scores, several factors must be taken into consideration when determining if a race is over-represented in the disparity index scores. Christian County is geographically located between two Missouri counties, which are both the annual destination of millions of tourists. With today's economy, the most popular travel destinations in the Midwest, Wonders of Wildlife and Branson area attractions, have seen an increase in non-local tourism. These non-residents travel on the roadways to their desti- nations on the primary roadways within Christian County. Vehicle stops conducted on these primary roadways will predictably be operated by a non-resident and would be more racially diverse.

As the County Seat, Clayton is home to the St. Louis County courts, Government Center, the Justice Center, and many other social services. Clayton is also home to major corporations, two universities, and a destination for restaurant and retail businesses. As a hub of economic, governmental, and educational activity for the entire region, Clayton welcomes diverse visitors to the community on a daily basis. The daytime population of Clayton increases substantially due to the activities and amenities mentioned above. Clayton also borders two diverse communities in the City of St. Louis and University City. Based on these characteristics, the use of Clayton's residential population is an inadequate method for measuring disparities in traffic stops. It's also important to note that minorities made up a disproportionate percentage of front-line workers required to travel to their job site during the pandemic. In 2021, no complaints of racial profiling were received against Clayton officers. Traffic stops in Clayton resulted in a very low rate of Resisting Arrest charges, which validates the officers' professional demeanor and ability to de-escalate. Clayton officers receive training to prevent the arbitrary use of investigative stops, pretextual stops, consent searches, and intrusive questions. Clayton officers also receive training in Implicit Bias and Procedural Justice.

The City of Clinton is located on the main route between the Kansas City metropolitan area and the City of Spring- field. Consequently, a large number of non-Clinton residents pass through the City of Clinton. In fact, 75% of all traffic stops made by the Clinton Police Department involve non-resident drivers. In addition, 37% of all traffic stops made by the Clinton Police Department take place on state highways. Given the large number of non-resident drivers stopped on state highways for traffic violations, any comparisons utilizing the population of the City of Clinton are inherently flawed.

In regards to the 2021 Vehicle Stops Report, I wish to provide the following information to help the reader better understand the data as it relates to the City of Creve Coeur. The residential demographics of Creve Coeur is not reflective of the motoring public demographics within the city limits. This is because the City of Creve Coeur is situated in the middle of St. Louis County and within our city limits are some of the most utilized roadways in the metro area. Additionally, the city is the home to several major corporations and medical facilities that draw people from all over the metropolitan area, boosting the daytime population to approximately 50,000 persons. Since only 10% of all motorists stopped by the Creve Coeur Police Department in 2021 were residents of Creve Coeur, perhaps

a more accurate metric for measuring the disparity index may be found by using population data from St. Louis County. When using this data, the disparity rate for vehicle stops involving Black motorists is 1.11. Determining the racial makeup of the motoring public in Creve Coeur is very complex, thus obtaining an accurate disparity index is difficult. However, an argument can be made that the motoring public in Creve Coeur is much more reflective of the St. Louis County population in general. Therefore, the reader should be cautioned that solely using the vehicle stop data provided in the Vehicle Stops Report, without taking into account other community dynamics, may lead to inaccurate conclusions.

The City of Des Peres Department of Public Safety maintains the data developed by the Attorney General continues to be flawed in that the device of comparing the racial distribution of traffic stops and residential population results in a comparison that is uninformative and misleading. The City of Des Peres is the crossroads for two of the bus- iest highways in St. Louis County, Interstate 270 and Manchester Rd (Highway 100). In addition, our community is a major retail hub serving people throughout the region. The truly relevant data would be the comparison of traffic stops with the racial diversity on all roadways within the city (and not just and analysis based on residential population). Unfortunately, this data was not developed or referenced by the Attorney General's office. The Des Peres Department of Public Safety is committed to just and unbiased policing and administration of the law. Our department does not engage in discriminatory practices, and the department, and city, would not tolerate it if it were to occur. Any reference to the contrary due to the data compiled in the current manner for the Attorney General's report is unwarranted and erroneous.

The Ellisville Police Department has a much greater diverse population than is reflected in our residential statistics. Manchester and Clarkson road sees 1.5 million cars per month travelling through the City of Ellisville. As such, the population of persons who travel through our jurisdiction more accurately reflects the overall metropolitan area population than using simply Ellisville residential population. Leaving this metric out of the overall equation skews all data and does not accurately reflect the true ratio of stops relative to population. In fact if taken with the metropolitan statistical representation the actual number of stops of minority members of the community is below average.
Our jurisdiction includes a main US Highway that would show different demographics than our actual population.

The City of Eureka is a very unique small city along Interstate 44 that traditionally attracts about 1 million visitors annually. 2019 tourism estimates suggest between 1 and 1.5 million visitors/tourist visited Eureka that year. 2021 estimates was significantly less as we came out of COVID-19 protocols but still higher than resident populations. Eureka is a family-friendly tourist destination that attracts visitors from across the country and represents a di- verse cross-section of the United States population. The Eureka Police Department has well established policies prohibiting racial profiling and biased based decisions. All officers receive yearly training on state mandated racial profiling, implicit bias for law enforcement and de-escalation for law enforcement officers. All training is from a licensed/approved provider of continuing education. Department processes include supervisory oversight of officer reports, citizen contacts, and traffic enforcement. Again, Eureka is a relatively small community that is also the home of Six Flags St. Louis amusement park, Hidden Valley Ski resort and a hub for many state and county parks in the area. Daily population often grows to 20,000 and above normal as does vehicle traffic. Our traffic counts significantly increase with increased visitors as Eureka has no mass transit options.

Fair Grove, Missouri is a residential community approximately 14 miles north of Springfield, Missouri. The major highway traveling through Fair Grove is U.S. 65. This is highway is traveled mostly by citizens traveling to and from work daily to Springfield. A second reason this is the most traveled roadway is tourist traveling to and from Branson, Missouri. For these reasons a large amount of traffic stops are conducted to promote highway safety. The population of Fair Grove is approximately 1,500. Most of the citizens are middle class Americans.

The City of Frontenac experiences a large number of drivers from outside our jurisdiction passing through on a regular basis; this is due in part to having a highly traveled section of Interstate-64, a major shopping mall, and the city being located near the geographic center of St. Louis County.

Galena is the county seat for the County of Stone. Though Galena population is small and not diverse, we have citizens of the county of numerous races and ethnic groups. These citizens travel to the city to conduct county business. Furthermore, Galena is a main throughfare to the popular tourist destination of Branson. Two main state highways pass through the city. This provides the city with traffic from tourist throughout the state and the country. Galena is a popular destination for people floating the James River. Galena has three businesses that provide canoe rentals and camping. These reasons contribute to the results that my skew the city's results.

Gladstone is considered an “inner tier” suburb in the Kansas City Metropolitan Statistical Area. It is completely surrounded by Kansas City, MO, a densely populated urban center with significantly different demographics than Gladstone. Kansas City's white population is approximately 60% as opposed to Gladstone's ¿82% white popula- tion. While Gladstone is primarily residential, it does have vibrant and active retail areas. These retail districts include big box stores such as Walmart and Home Depot in addition to grocery stores and other retail businesses. Because of these districts and the overall location of Gladstone, there are multiple thoroughfares and arterial roads running through the city. These roads are used by many residents of surrounding municipalities to connect to the multiple state and interstate highways that run just outside of Gladstone's city limits in addition to patronizing our businesses. When viewed from that context, it appears that at any one time, the majority of people operating vehicles inside the corporate city limits of Gladstone are not its residents. As demonstrated by submitted statis- tics, approximately 71% of the stops conducted by our officers are on vehicles operated by non-residents. Since the numbers clearly show the vast majority of individuals contacted by Gladstone officers during vehicle stops are not residents, comparing the demographics of these drivers to the demographics of Gladstone's residents will result in flawed conclusions. Finally, as a CALEA accredited organization, Gladstone PD is continuously providing training to its members on such topics as bias-based policing and legal updates to ensure a well-trained, professional police force.

The State's report indicated a driving-age population demographic for the City of Grandview as 51.2% White, 35.5% Black, 11.8% Hispanic and 1.6% Asian respectively. However, the city's true driving demographics are affected by several major influences which are not reflected in the census data. The first is that the City of Grandview is a first tier city to Kansas City, MO which is bisected by 149/U.S. 71 highway; a major corridor direct to the inner city which maintains a high minority population. Much of our evening traffic is made up of motorists traveling to and from the inner city. Secondly, in 2010, the International House of Prayer opened their international university campus in the city. This campus brings in several thousand racially diverse students, which operate motor vehicles on our city streets on a daily basis but which are not represented in the census driving population data. Finally, since the 2020 census, the Grand Summit Apartments located on MO 150 highway (a major traffic corridor) have completed a large expansion, increasing by over 300 apartments. These apartments have since been filled and another large expansion is planned in the next year. The occupants of these apartments are racially diverse and are also not reflected in the

census driving population data.

Community dynamics such as a population disparities and number of major roadways could skew data on vehicle stops. Other factors are that drivers may not live within Greene County, but are traveling through due to numerous tourist attractions in and around our area. With various major highways (13, 65, 125, 160, 60, 266, 413 and 360), as well as Interstate 44 within Greene County, there are thousands of transient drivers and occupants on our roads every day.

The City of Hannibal is unique as it is located off two major highways, US 61 and US 36. These highways put Han- nibal in the crossroads of major thoroughfares which lead to major cities like St. Louis, Chicago, Kansas City, and Minneapolis. During a normal business day thousands of cars travel on Hannibal's roadways and stop at businesses in our community. This influx of traffic can change the demographic makeup of the community which would not be accurately reflected in the American Community Survey estimates.

Reference Hawk Point Police Department and the current statistic report, we have no errors. Our agency is under a new administration and our policing is showing just that. Due to some local complaints we have received reference traffic violations and careless driving, we have been and plan on being out consistently to show our dedication to our community and keeping it safe.

The City of Hazelwood Missouri encompasses 16.76 square miles and is one of the largest cities in St. Louis County and home to 25,458 (2020 Decennial Census) residents and more than 1,000 businesses, including 12 Fortune 500 com- panies, six Fortune 1,000 companies and 10 corporate headquarters. The City of Hazelwood contains 159.5 centerline miles of roadway that include Highways 67, 170, 270, and 370 with an estimated 267,000 vehicles traveling through the city limits daily. Every weekday, nearly 415,000 motorists travel through our city on the 2nd most voluminous interchange in the State (Interstates 64/40/270). Our motoring public is more reflective of the demographics of the State and St. Louis County, which are comprised of African American populations of 11.5% and 24.9% respectively (US Census Bureau). The stop data for Town & Country Police indicate over 78% of stops occurred on either the interstates, US Highway or State Highway, of which, 91 % were initiated for hazardous moving violations. Overall, 22% of the stops were African American drivers. The natural implication of this statistic is that the ratio of the race of motorists stopped for violations would reflect state and county demographics, not the residential population of the City of Town & Country. The Town & Country Police is committed to fair and impartial application of the law while attempting to maintain a safe environment for all people who travel in and through our City limits. A careful examination of the data indicates that our practice matches this policy.

Henry County is located between Kansas City and Springfield, along MO-Highway 7 and MO-Highway 13. Both are 4-lane highways and major travelling corridors between these metropolitan areas throughout the year, which creates noticeable increases in traffic volume. Henry County is also located on Truman Lake, which includes federally- maintained campgrounds, privately-owned campgrounds and numerous weekend lake homes, and is bordered by Benton County (east) and St. Clair County (south), which are both also located on Truman Lake and are each comprised of several federally-maintained and privately-owned campgrounds and numerous weekend lake homes, as well. MO-7 and MO-13 connect Henry County with Benton and St. Clair Counties and are major travelling corridors to these lake areas. These roadways are also major corridors through Henry County to other vacation and weekend locations, which include Lake of the Ozarks and Branson. According to annual statistics compiled by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, the federally-maintain campgrounds accounted for over 350,000 visitors to Henry County each year. These same statistics revealed that the federally-maintained campgrounds in Benton County accounted for over 800,000 visitors and in St. Clair County accounted for over 200,000 visitors, and a large majority of these visitors travel either MO-7 or MO-13, which is the most direct routes of travel, in order to access these camping facilities. Henry County's population shows over a 300% increase, due to the number of persons utilizing these campgrounds alone.

Just a quick response to our VSR report. We believe that your population estimates for 2021 Black, Hispanic, and Asian populations are grossly in error in being over reported. These numbers will greatly skew any results. Also, we are a small town that has a State highway running through it. We are also the County seat which generates a daytime transient population 3 to 5 times higher than our population because of the Courthouse, Jail, County government, and a Junior College. There are also countywide and regional wide events at our Fairgrounds all sum- mer such as the rodeo, horseshow, County Fair, tractor pull, auction, Fall festival, and etc. These events push our transient population 9 to 10 times our local population. All of these conditions have a great potential to affect our traffic stop statistics based only on our population breakdown. Thank you for your consideration of these variants.
We do have to major thoroughfares within the Village of Hillsdale, those are: Kienlen Ave. and Lucas & Hunt Road.

The City of Holts Summit experiences a large transient/motorist population on any given day; as thousands of motorist travel along US HWY 54; which passes through our town, to larger cities, vacation destinations, and other major Interstate/Roadways. Of the 183 stops involving African American's; 142 were identified as being non- residents of our jurisdiction.

In Howell County, we have a large number of motorists passing through as the highway between Jonesboro Arkansas and places like Springfield Missouri run through our county. With being a large hub county leading to major cities, there is an increase in traffic thus leading to increased numbers of traffic offenses. Our deputies try to ensure safe passages for all motorists including residents here in our county. We have increased patrol to ensure all traffic laws are being enforced.

Reduced manpower, modified COVID schedules, and modified COVID work/activity procedures significantly im- pacted 2021's data compared to previous years. Independence is the fifth-largest city in Missouri, county seat and immediate suburb of Kansas City, with a population of 117,217. Ten major Interstates, highways, and roadways allow direct access to the multiple racially diverse Kansas City metropolitan attractions located in the community; Harry S. Truman Presidential Library and historic home, Cable Dahmer Arena , Super-Regional Independence Cen- ter Shopping Mall (2nd largest in KC Metro area with approximately 12 million annual visitors), 39th Street retail area, AMC 20 movie theater complex, Crysler Baseball Stadium, Community of Christ International Headquarters, colleges, community festivals, and multi-cultural dining venues. There are 49% of all traffic stops conducted by the Department in 2021 involved residents from other jurisdictions. Significant traffic safety efforts are conducted along the major roadways and additional funding is provided for enforcement through state funded Hazardous Moving Violation grants, DWI Enforcement grants and Occupant Protection grants. The Independence Police Department is a professional law enforcement organization that is committed to impartial, competent, and courteous service to the public without regard to race, creed, or gender. Officers receive annual training on cultural challenges and issues by ensuring and maintaining accountability in part through the integrity of the racial profiling documentation process in compliance with Missouri State Statute.

Also, in regards to our response. It should be noted that a lot of our traffic is pass through traffic to and from our adjoining City of Cape Girardeau. Our daytime population and commuters through our city exceed 30,000 according to our last traffic count on our main highway.

Jasper County has a population of 121,328 persons. Jasper County is home to Missouri Southern State University which boasts a widely diverse ethnic population which is not counted in the census. Further, Jasper County has two major interstate arteries intersecting, Interstate 44 and Interstate 49. Further, Jasper County is home to an historic stretch of Route 66 which attracts people from many different states and countries. Jasper County is also home to the largest Asian religious festival in the United States, where in August of each year more than 80,000 Vietnamese visit our county seat for a week. For these reasons we often see a population that is not necessarily represented by our census numbers.

Unlike the population in the cities and urban and rural communities of Missouri, the Kansas City International Air- port, as a business, sees a large diversity of people on any given day through its passengers, tenants and employees of the various businesses on the Airport property. Due to this diverse population, there is the possibility that the report- ing numbers for this agency may be slightly skewed, and may not reflect the population of the communities around it.

The City of Kearney is located in Clay County and is a fast-growing suburb of Kansas City, which is evident by all the new commercial businesses and housing editions being built. Interstate 35 runs through Kearney and has one entrance/exit with a high-volume truck stop utilized by commercial drivers and numerous convenience stores used by interstate travelers and local residents. Another interstate entrance/exit is currently under construction and with that will come more businesses. Kearney also has two busy State Highways, MO-92 and MO-33 which are utilized daily by the 24,000 plus people who live within a 5-mile radius of Kearney City Limits. Kearney has several events, parks, and an amphitheater which draws in large crowds throughout the year.

Kirksville is home to two major universities whose enrollment data is not totally reflected in the census figure. Truman State University has 720 students listed other than white. They also have an additional 388 international students not included in the 720. A.T. Still University reports that approximately 30% of their students are listed as non-white. The majority of ATSU students, 99%, come from outside the Kirksville area. The City of Kirksville has also seen a significant increase of families from the Democratic Republic of the Congo in the last decade. As an example, the Kirksville RIII School District reports 120 students enrolled in K-12 for the 2021-2022 school year who are Congolese.
